data.title: Berkshire Hathaway CEO Abel stresses technology opportunity at annual meeting
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) CEO Greg Abel reviewed results for the investment conglomerate's sprawling collection of businesses, noting that technology, including artificial intelligence, "touches the whole franchise of Berkshire."

The new CEO emphasized the importance of leveraging technology to fuel growth. At some point, management realized, "We're going to be a builder of technology, rather than just a buyer of technology," he said in his first annual meeting as CEO.

In terms of AI, "it has to be additive to our businesses. We're not going to have AI just to have AI," Abel said.

Earlier, the company delivered Q1 operating earnings of $11.3B, climbing 18% from the year-ago period, helped by a 29% jump in its insurance underwriting profit. Its cash pile grew 6.5% Q/Q to $397.4B.

In his discussion of Q1 results, Abel attributed part of the company's improvement in insurance underwriting profit to the $860M after-tax charge related to California wildfires the company recorded in 2025 results. By contrast, 2026 results didn't reflect any catastrophic events.

The CEO stated that its Geico business is focused on restarting growth. It has to "get the price to risk right," retain customers, and grow its policies in force, he said. There's much to do there. Geico's policies in force, or PIF, rose 2% in Q1, while its closest competitor, Progressive's (PGR) PIF climbed 11%. "It's not going to be easy to just restart the growth engine," he said.

Abel also mentioned Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.B) $1.8B investment in Tokio Marine (TKOMY) (TKOMF), calling it a long-term strategic partnership. 

The company's railroad, BNSF, has made progress, but there's "a lot more to be done," he said, pointing to the unit's operating margin expansion to 34.4% in Q1 from 32.1% in last year's Q1. 

"We see a lot of opportunity here to become better," he added. "That's going to require a step-change in how we approach our operations." Technology will be key, he said, as some competitors have gapped up its use.

"How can we move our cars quicker and meet our customer expectations?" he asks. That also requires determining the best use of its resources and employees. 

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) Energy has made progress in its litigation position. In April, the Oregon Court of Appeals ruled in favor of its PacifiCorp utility, which allowed the company to recover about $1B of securities it had posted, Abel said. "We have reset the stage," he added.

Abel pointed out that the company's net cash and short-term securities are $380.2B, which is less than the headline number of $397.4B. The difference is in $17.2B payable for purchases of U.S. Treasury bills, he said.

The CEO said its Occidental Chemical purchase resulted in a little more than a $3B decrease in results. Still, he's “very pleased” with the transaction.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Earnings scoreboard: 11 of 12 consumer staple names beat earnings estimates this week
data.description:
Consumer staples companies delivered a broadly positive and resilient set of earnings last week, with pricing power continuing to support revenue growth even as volumes remained under pressure.

Broadly, the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) has outperformed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) YTD, rising about 8.35% versus roughly 5.6% for the broader market, driven by a clear defensive rotation to staples.

Out of 35 consumer staple names that are part of the S&P 500 (SPY), 12 reported earnings last week, with most delivering a beat on estimates. 11 out of 12 firms beat EPS estimates, while 1 reported in-line numbers.

Based on the revenue, 10 companies beat revenue estimates, while 2 companies fell short of estimates.

Of the 12 consumer companies of the S&P 500 index, 10 reported Y/Y earnings growth, and 9 saw topline growth.

Here is a list of the latest quarterly reports in the consumer staples sector this week:

Coca-Cola (KO) Q1 earnings report comfortably beat expectations across nearly every key metric, and the move higher rippled through the broader consumer staples space. Global unit case volume grew 3% during the quarter, with all segments positive.

Altria Group (MO) topped Q1 estimates with revenue up 3.2% to $5.43B and adjusted EPS at $1.32, as higher pricing offset declining shipment volumes. The gains were driven by pricing power across cigarettes and oral tobacco, which helped counter ongoing volume declines amid weak consumer demand.

Colgate-Palmolive (CL) topped consensus estimates of analysts with its Q1 earnings report. Its revenue was up 8.4% to $5.32B. Non-GAAP EPS was reported at $0.97 vs. $0.94 consensus and $0.91 a year ago. The company said its leadership in toothpaste continued with its global market share at 41.1. YTD, while its leadership in manual toothbrushes continued with its global market share at 32.6% YTD.

Hershey (HSY) - The chocolate-maker set a profit guidance for the year with a midpoint that is less than Wall Street’s estimates, though it beat Q1 estimates. In its candy, mint, and gum segment, retail takeaway increased 8.1% despite higher prices, although Hershey’s market share declined Y/Y due to increased competition and timing of merchandising programs.

Shares of Clorox (CLX) came under pressure after the company’s fiscal Q3 results and outlook for the year suggested continued headwinds for the consumer products company. It's Non-GAAP EPS of $1.64 beat by $0.10, while its topline was at $1.67B, in line with estimates, but showed no growth Y/Y.

Mondelez International (MDLZ) saw Q1 organic sales jump 3%, beating estimates, with revenue up ~8%, driven by strong growth in emerging markets. However, adjusted EPS fell 14.9% Y/Y to $0.67, missing expectations, as margin pressure from higher input costs offset pricing gains.

At the industry level, 4 household products, 3 food products, 2 beverages, 1 personal care product, 1 tobacco, and 1 distribution & retail company reported earnings this week.

Among the consumer staple names that are reporting earnings next week are Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), Kenvue (KVUE), Kraft Heinz (KHC), and Tyson Foods (TSN).

data.title: U.S. approves $8.6B military sales to Middle Eastern allies
data.description:
The Trump administration has approved military sales worth more than $8.6B to America’s Middle Eastern allies, Israel, Qatar, Kuwait, and the UAE, benefiting weapons manufacturers such as Northrop Grumman (NOC) and RTX (RTX).

The U.S. Department of State announced on Friday that the Secretary of State Marco ​Rubio waived the congressional review requirements for the transactions after determining that there was an emergency necessitating immediate sales to those countries.

The approval comes amid a fragile ceasefire that is taking effect in the Middle East following the onset of the U.S. and Israel’s military campaign against Iran in late February.  

Per the announcement, the approved deals to Qatar include the sale of Patriot air and missile defense replenishment services worth $4.0B and Advanced Precision Kill Weapon Systems (APKWS) worth $992.4M.

Other approved sales include an integrated battle command system costing $2.5B for Kuwait and APKWS worth $992.4M and $147.4M for Israel and the UAE, respectively.

BAE Systems (BAESY) (BAESF) was named the principal contractor in the APKWS sales, and Northrop Grumman (NOC) was named a principal contractor for the Kuwaiti sale.

RTX (RTX) and Lockheed Martin (LMT) were the principal contractors for the sales of the integrated battle command system to Kuwait and the Patriot air and missile defense replenishment to Qatar.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: AMD, Meta and T-Mobile among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday after investors witnessed strong corporate earnings along with retreating oil prices.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

AMD downgraded ahead of earnings

Northland Capital downgraded AMD (AMD) to Market Perform from Outperform.

“We expect AMD to beat and raise estimates when it reports next week, but the results will likely not be as robust as Intel's,” analyst Gus Richard wrote in a note to clients. “While AMD will benefit from price increases, we believe AMD needs to continue spending heavily on R&D to catch up with NVDA.”

The brokerage kept its PT of $260 on the stock.

“We believe the CY27 consensus needs to come down, as we expect AI infrastructure spending to decline in CY27,” Richard added.

Meta downgraded after AI spending surge

J.P. Morgan’s Doug Anmuth downgraded Meta Platforms (META) to Neutral from Overweight following its Q1 results and increased capex spending.

Anmuth said that “shares could remain pressured as investors look for greater clarity on agentic products and how Muse models will help drive incremental revenue beyond advertising.”

“We look for greater clarity into the path to returns on AI spending beyond the core ad business and believe building, iterating, scaling, and monetizing new products and experiences will take time,” the brokerage added.

T-Mobile receives upgrade on Q1 results

T-Mobile (TMUS) earned an upgrade at Oppenheimer on Q1 results, free cash flow, and M&A opportunities. Analyst Timothy Horan expects T-Mobile to focus on closing the 20% price discount with peers while maintaining cost controls to keep expenses flat year-over-year, largely by relying more heavily on artificial intelligence.

Increases to its free cash flow to an “industry-leading 13% per year” on strong margins and buybacks also support Horan’s upgrade to Outperform.

“Long-term guidance is also likely conservative with room for upside from M&A and synergies,” Horan says, adding that the current 11% free cash flow yield and 5-year low of ~8x EV/EBITDA create “an attractive entry point.”

UBS downgraded Nucor (NUE) on valuation concerns. The brokerage lowered the rating to Neutral from Buy with a $224 price target following the company’s Q1 results, with shares up ~36% since March 20—even before the company's strong Q1 print—and now trading at ~7.7x estimated Q2 2027-Q1 2028 EV/EBITDA, the highest multiple vs. consensus near-term estimates in 10 years, analyst Andrew Jones said.

Bank of America Securities upgraded Radian Group (RDN) to Buy from Underperform and raised its price target to $43 from $35 based on Radian's transformation into a global, diversified specialty insurer from a U.S. mortgage insurer. 

Citi raised its estimates for Palantir (PLTR) on rising momentum for the enterprise software company but also lowered its price target on the stock to $210 from $260.

“We expect continued momentum in Q1, both USG and commercial, and more progress Internationally, following the large renewals with Airbus and Stellantis,” analyst Tyler Radke wrote, adding that the brokerage is raising FY27/28 estimates more than 500bps ahead of consensus.

“However, the recent multiple compression in software cut our TP to $210 [from $260] (implying ~48x FY27 EV/Sales; ~87x EV/FCF). We reiterate our Buy/HR rating, as we believe PLTR remains one of the top AI beneficiaries as AI continues to proliferate across enterprises, as noted at AI Summit recently,” Radke added.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway's Buffett underscores his support for Abel
data.description:
Early in the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) annual meeting, Chairman Warren Buffett reinforced his support of  Greg Abel, saying his decision to name Abel CEO "is the right decision."

"Greg is doing everything I did and then some," he said, adding that Abel is also doing many things better than he did.

He also pointed to the 10-year anniversary of Berkshire acquiring a stake in Apple (AAPL). The company spent $35B at the time. That's turned into $185B, including dividends and realized and unrealized appreciation, "and I didn't have to do a damn thing," he said.

Abel started alone on the dais at the company's 60th annual meeting, after Warren Buffett helmed the proceedings for 59 years. The new CEO honored his predecessor by “retiring” a jersey with Buffett's name and the number 60 on it for the number of years the legendary investor was Berkshire's CEO. Another jersey was lifted to the arena's rafters with Munger and 45 for Charlie Munger, who was Buffett's business partner for 45 years.

Earlier, the company delivered Q1 operating earnings of $11.3B, climbing 18% from the year-ago period, helped by a  29% jump in its insurance underwriting profit. Its cash pile grew 6.5% Q/Q to $397.4B. The company bought back $234.2M of

data.title: Visa, Blue Owl in gainers; Mastercard, insurance stocks among losers: week's financials wrap
data.description:
Financial stocks were rising in a week that saw the Federal Reserve hold its target range for the federal funds rate but imply an easing bias in the forward guidance.

Meanwhile, earnings continued to impact stock prices.

State Street Financial Sel Sec SPDR ETF (XLF) added 0.97% week-over-week to close at $51.92.

Visa (V) was the biggest gainer of the week among megacap stocks, rising 6.01% to close at $328.03.

The payment network stock bounced after fiscal second-quarter earnings handily topped Wall Street expectations, as consumers continued to spend, and the company announced a new stock buyback program.

The week also saw Visa add five more blockchains to its global stablecoin settlement pilot, expanding its multi-chain settlement capabilities to nine blockchains.

HSBC Holdings (HSBC) and Royal Bank of Canada (RY) followed, adding 2.52% and 2.40% to end at $91.95 and $179.54, respectively.

Mastercard (MA) topped the losers even as the payment network delivered better-than-expected Q1 earnings and revenue.

The stock lost 1.73% of its value to close at $495.46.

April trends showed a slowdown in cross-border transaction growth.

For largecap stocks, Franklin Resources (BEN) (+10.03% W/W to $29.84) topped the weekly gainers. The stock was upgraded at Barclays after the second-quarter earnings release on improving fundamentals.

ORIX (IX) (+8.72% W/W to $33.05) and Blue Owl Capital (OWL) (+8.71% W/W to $9.98) were the next on the list.

Blue Owl Capital stock climbed after the asset manager delivered better-than-feared Q1 results, with performance holding up across its credit, real assets, and GP strategic capital strategies.

Notably, co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said that the company has sold about half of its stake in SpaceX ((SPACE)) at a $1.25T valuation, crystallizing roughly a tenfold return.

Galaxy Digital (GLXY) followed, advancing 8.09% to close at $28.11. The company exceeded the Wall Street estimates in terms of EPS and revenue even as concerns lingered over depreciation of digital asset prices.

Robinhood Markets (HOOD) (-13.04% W/W to $73.66) and Brown & Brown (BRO) (-12.55% W/W to $57.63) led the decliners as their quarterly earnings releases failed to impress the market. 

Twenty One Capital (XXI) (+14.62% W/W to $8.94), a company that engages in the Bitcoin-related business, topped the midcap gainers. Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) (+13.37% W/W to $36.45) was the next on the list.

The stocks benefited from a rally in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) prices this week. The world's largest digital currency inched closer to the $80K mark.

In the category, insurance stocks Lemonade (LMND) (-13.77% W/W to $56.66), Baldwin Insurance Group (BWIN) (-12.34% W/W to $22.03), and Ryan Specialty Holdings (RYAN) (-10.92% W/W to $30.83) led the weekly losers.

Baldwin had filed a proxy statement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for its 2026 annual meeting of stockholders last week.

Meanwhile, Lemonade and Ryan dropped despite reporting quarterly earnings above analyst estimates.

BRC Group Holdings (RILY) (+17.54% W/W to $8.98), CoinShares (CSHR) (+13.14% W/W to $6.20), Ethos Technologies (LIFE) (+11.21% W/W to $18.65), LendingTree (TREE) (-19.58% W/W to $38.81), and Grupo Supervielle (SUPV) (-12.14% W/W to $7.89) were the significant smallcap movers.

data.title: Earnings Scoreboard: 82% of S&P 500 reporting firms top EPS estimates as 78% of firms post Y/Y earnings growth
data.description:
S&P 500 earnings accelerated this week, with over 178 companies from financial, healthcare, and industrial sectors reporting. Key companies included the "Magnificent Seven" giants such as Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT). Results from Coca-Cola (KO), Starbucks (SBUX), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) helped assess corporate margins amid changing economic conditions.

Earnings Roundup:

Bottom Line: Out of the 178 companies reporting, 147 exceeded EPS expectations, 19 fell short, and 12 met estimates. Notably, 139 firms posted year-over-year earnings growth.

Top Line: Performance remained robust on the revenue front, with 143 companies topping estimates while 35 missed. On a year-over-year basis, 157 of the companies reported revenue expansion.

 

 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week:

Microsoft (MSFT): Reported revenue of $82.89B and an EPS of $4.27, both beating analyst estimates, while the company maintained its upbeat cloud growth guidance.

Alphabet (GOOG): Revenue reached $109.9B with an EPS of $5.11, beating expectations on both metrics and prompting the board to maintain its positive AI-driven outlook.

Meta Platforms (META): Posted revenue of $56.31B and an EPS of $10.44, surpassing consensus estimates, though the stock faced pressure after management raised its capital expenditure guidance for the year.

Apple (AAPL): Q2 revenue came in at $111.18B with an EPS of $2.01, beating estimates. iPhone revenue was $56.99B, marginally above estimates, and overall product revenue reached $80.21B, also slightly exceeding forecasts. Mac revenue was $8.4B, up 5.7% year-over-year, surpassing expectations as well.

Amazon (AMZN): Q1 reported revenue of $181.52B and an EPS of $2.78, both significant beats driven by AWS acceleration. It anticipates Q2 revenue between $194.0B and $199.0B, surpassing the $189.5B consensus. It expects operating income of $20.0B to $24.0B, below the $22.9B consensus, assuming Prime Day occurs in Q2.

Starbucks (SBUX): Q2 beats on both revenue ($9.53B) and EPS ($0.50), raises full-year 2026 guidance for comparable store sales and earnings.

Ford (F): Beat EPS estimates at $0.66 on revenue of $39.82B. Buoyed by this momentum, the automaker raised its full-year adjusted EBIT guidance to a range of $8.5 billion to $10.5 billion.

Verizon (VZ): delivered a mixed first-quarter performance, posting a non-GAAP EPS of $1.28 that topped analyst estimates by $0.07. While revenue grew 2.7% year-over-year to $34.4 billion, it fell short of expectations by $490 million. It raised its 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to a growth range of 5.0% to 6.0%, signaling that total retail postpaid phone net additions are now expected to land in the upper half of its previously stated target of 750,000 to one million.

AbbVie (ABBV): delivered a strong top-line performance for the first quarter, with revenue climbing 12.4% year-over-year to $15 billion, surpassing analyst estimates. Non-GAAP EPS of $2.65 narrowly missed expectations. Despite the slight earnings miss, the company raised its full-year 2026 adjusted diluted EPS guidance to a range of $14.08–$14.28.

Caterpillar (CAT): surpasses Q1 earnings and revenue expectations and raises its full-year revenue forecast, driven by demand for power generation equipment.

Exxon Mobil (XOM): after exceeding Q1 adjusted earnings expectations. However, unadjusted net profit fell to a five-year low due to disrupted shipments from the Middle East war and negative timing effects from financial derivatives. Revenue was $85.14B, missing estimates by $150M. 

Visa (V): reported strong fiscal Q2 earnings that surpassed Wall Street predictions. The company reported a fiscal Q2 2026 adjusted EPS of $3.31, exceeding the analyst estimate, and showed net revenue of $11.2B, which also beat the consensus. Visa’s board approved a new $20B stock buyback program. For FY2026, Visa expects EPS growth in the low teens and net revenue growth in the low double digits.

Earnings next week

Next week marks a critical stretch for the S&P 500 as about 120 companies report first-quarter earnings, offering a comprehensive look at the state of the American consumer and healthcare sectors. Major reports are expected from CVS Health (CVS), Pfizer (PFE), Kraft Heinz (KHC), and Marriott (MAR).

Beyond these consumer and health giants, the tech sector will be in focus with highly anticipated results from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Palantir (PLTR), and Super Micro Computer (SMCI).

Financial and payment platforms such as PayPal (PYPL), Coinbase (COIN), and KKR (KKR) are also on the docket, alongside industrial and energy leaders like Eaton (ETN), Cummins (CMI), and Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

More on markets:

data.title: Insider trades: Abbott, UnitedHealth and ServiceNow among notable names
data.description:
Daniel J. Starks, a director at Abbott Laboratories (NYSE: ABT), acquired 10,000 shares of the company’s common stock on April 27, 2026, for a total value of $926,537, bringing his total shares  holding to 6.75M. Separately, EVP and CFO Philip P. Boudreau disclosed several share-related transactions dated April 23, 2026, including an open-market purchase of 2,200 shares at $91.50 each, held indirectly through his spouse. Separately, Boudreau reports 71,172 common shares held directly after these transactions. The purchases come at a notable time, with Abbott stock trading near its 52-week low of $, reflecting a decline of roughly 29% so far this year.

S&P Global (SPGI) CEO Martina Cheung purchased 2,322 shares of the company’s stock on Wednesday, April 29, at an average price of $429.93 per share, amounting to a total investment of $998,297.46. Following the transaction, Cheung’s holdings increased to 27,518 shares, valued at approximately $11.83 million, marking a 9.22% rise in her ownership stake.

Scott Tozier, a director at International Paper (IP), acquired 10,000 shares of the company on May 1, 2026, for an estimated $313,009. Following the transaction, he owns a total of 10,025 shares of International Paper.

President and CEO Christopher L. Winfrey purchased a total of 6,936 Charter (CHTR) Class A shares through open-market transactions on April 28, 2026, including a separate entry for 3,468 shares held by his spouse, at a weighted average price of about $172, lifting his direct stake to 74,409 shares. In a separate filing, director Wade Davis acquired 5,728 shares on April 28, 2026, at $173.72 per share. Additionally, Nair Balan purchased 1,000 shares at $175.46, bringing his total stake to 12,029 shares.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) executive Patrick Hugh Conway, CEO of Optum, disclosed an open-market sale of 800 shares of the company’s common stock at $355.00 per share. Following the transaction, he directly owns 17,804.991 shares.

Jacqueline P. Canney, Chief People & AI Enablement Officer of ServiceNow (NOW), sold 8,927 shares of common stock on April 24, 2026, at $89.601 per share, for a total of $799,868. Following the transaction, she directly held 29,531 shares of the company’s common stock.

Halliburton (HAL) director Tobi M. Young disclosed sales of common stock and holdings in restricted stock units. On April 30, 2026, Young sold a total of 6,125 shares in open-market transactions at prices of $41.72 and $41.725 per share.

data.title: Berkshire earnings rebound in Q1 thanks to insurance-underwriting
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) announced its Q1 2026 financials on Saturday, indicating a rise in its operating earnings following a sharp drop in the preceding quarter as its insurance-underwriting business offset a decline in insurance-investment income.

In Q4 2025, Berkshire (BRK.B) posted $10.2B in operating earnings with a ~30% drop as after-tax earnings from its insurance underwriting and insurance investment income fell ~54% YoY and 25% YoY, respectively.

However, the Omaha, Nebraska-based company reported $11.3B in operating earnings with ~18% YoY growth for the latest quarter, which was the first under Greg Abel, who succeeded billionaire investor Warren Buffett as CEO in January.

The rise in operating earnings reflected Berkshire’s (BRK.B) Insurance—underwriting business, which added $1.7B with a ~29% YoY growth, thanks mainly to a lack of significant catastrophe events during the quarter.

However, its insurance-investment income slipped ~7% YoY to $2.7B, primarily due to a decline in interest income attributed to lower interest rates.

Its railroad business, BNSF, which runs one of the largest railroad systems in North America, added $1.4B with a ~13% YoY rise driven by higher revenues and operational efficiencies. Despite a mixed performance, Berkshire's (BRK.B) manufacturing, service, and retailing operations added $3.2B with ~5% YoY growth.

Berkshire Hathaway Energy Company generated $1.1B with a ~2% YoY rise, benefiting from higher earnings from the natural gas pipeline businesses and federal income tax credits, which offset lower earnings from U.S. utilities and other energy units.

The conglomerate's financial position continued to strengthen as its cash, cash equivalents, and short-term securities stood at $397.4B as of March 31, implying a ~6% YoY growth amid nearly $8.1B in sales of equity securities on a net basis.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway Q1 operating earnings +18% Y/Y, cash hits $397.4B
data.description:
• Berkshire Hathaway press release (BRK.A) (BRK.B): Q1 operating earnings of $11.3B (+17.7% Y/Y).
  • Net earnings per average equivalent Class A share of $7,027
  • Net earnings per average equivalent Class B share of $4.68
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term securities of $397.4B
  • SEC filing
